title: Entanglement Dynamics across a Monitored Quantum Point Contact
abstract: We compute the entanglement dynamics across a monitored quantum point contact, where particle losses are recorded on a given site, and demonstrate how this single-site local monitoring substantially reshapes the entanglement production. Contrary to the unitary case, where entanglement entropy grows logarithmically in time, here we find first a linear growth, up to a maximum value displaying volume-law scaling, and then a slow decay to zero, as the system empties out. We capture this crossover using a quasiparticle picture, where the first linear growth arises due to an emergent bias voltage established by the losses, which eventually decays away as the system depletes. We connect our results to studies of the Page curve and to experimentally relevant probes, via full counting statistics of charge transfer across a subregion, with only a single channel to unravel leading to a favorable scaling of the postselection overhead. Natural platforms for this setting include mesoscopic systems and ultracold atoms.
summary: This paper demonstrates that local monitoring of particle loss at a quantum point contact fundamentally changes entanglement growth from logarithmic to linear, followed by a decay. The study links these entanglement dynamics to charge depletion and provides an analytical quasiparticle framework to explain the transition.
main_problem: Investigating how continuous local monitoring of particle losses at a quantum point contact (QPC) reshapes the entanglement dynamics and charge transport compared to the unitary case.
main_result: Local monitoring transforms entanglement growth from logarithmic to a three-regime process: linear growth (volume-law), power-law decay, and eventual exponential decay to zero. This dynamics is driven by an emergent bias voltage created by the losses.
method: The authors use a quantum jump protocol described by a stochastic Schrodinger equation and a Lindblad master equation, supplemented by a quasiparticle picture and full counting statistics (FCS) analysis.
model_or_system: A quantum point contact connecting two metallic leads modeled as fermionic tight-binding chains with open boundary conditions, subject to a monitoring protocol that detects particle losses at a specific site.
key_observables: Entanglement entropy (S_L,R), particle imbalance, total charge depletion, and full counting statistics (FCS) of charge transfer.
important_parameters: Monitoring/dissipation rate (gamma/J), system size (L), and the hopping parameter (J).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled as free fermions; the quasiparticle picture assumes entanglement production is a binomial process of fermion detection/non-detection.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the system schematic; Figure 2 displays entanglement entropy dynamics and its volume-law scaling; Figure 3 illustrates particle imbalance and the relationship between entanglement and total particle number.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the monitored QPC model, presents the numerical results for entanglement and charge dynamics, develops a quasiparticle-based analytical framework to explain the observed regimes, and connects the findings to experimental probes like FCS.
why_interesting: It provides a deep connection between measurement-induced dynamics, non-equilibrium transport, and the Page curve, offering a bridge between mesoscopic physics and the study of open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Sketch of the system: two leads described by a tight-binding chain with hopping parameter J are connected via a quantum point contact opening according to f(t). A detector measures particle losses at the last site of the left lead with a rate 2γ.

Figure 2. Dynamics of the entanglement entropy between left and right lead. (a) Dynamics of the entanglement entropy for a chanin of 2L = 96 sites and several measurement strengths. The orange line corresponds to the unitary case γ/J = 0. (b) Comparison of the dynamics for diﬀerent system sizes at γ/J = 2. The peak of the entanglement entropy scales linearly with the system size. The dotted line ﬁts the initial linear regime. (c) Polynomial regime of the entanglement entropy at γ/J = 2, the solid black line represents a decay with a universal power (Jt)−0.56. The plot is in log-log scale. (d) Exponential regime at long times. The dashed lines represent the exponential ﬁt of the curves for...

Figure 3. (a) Dynamics of the particle imbalance between the two leads for diﬀerent sizes, and γ/J = 2.0.(b) Ratio between the entanglement entropy and total density for γ/J = 2. In the inset, the total number of particle is plotted as a function of nLL, and the polynomial and exponential regimes of the entanglement entropy and density emerge respectively from the ﬁts in dashed and dotted lines. (c) Entanglement entropy captured through the cumulants of the transported charge. The main plot considers cumulants C2, C4, while the inset considers solely C2. Results are shown for several truncations of the cumulant sum, as shown in legend.
